narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 26, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 1st decile, and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated a 53.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This prediction above 50% triggered the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 1st percentile, far below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at a critical stage. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which is stronger than the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward expecting a bar exam pass. Your family's upper-middle income also nudged things positively, as it's associated with better outcomes compared to lower brackets in the dataset. Your notably low LSAT score of 26—well under the average of about 37—likely played a supporting role in the overall risk assessment, though it wasn't the top driver here. Full-time enrollment, like most applicants at 93%, didn't sway the decision much either way. In essence, while your GPA and background provided some reassurance, your low Year 3 decile dominated, making you riskier than the typical admitted student who boasts mid-range deciles and higher test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a solid family—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model saw your academic trajectory as a red flag compared to peers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those deciles, or exploring part-time programs or prep courses proven to lift bar passage odds. With targeted improvements, you could reapply stronger next cycle and flip this prediction in your favor.
